era_context: The late reign of Shalmaneser IV of Assyria (ending 773 BCE), the early Eastern Zhou Dynasty in China during the Spring and Autumn period, and the Greek Archaic period. This year represents a period of relative stability in the Near East, with the Neo-Assyrian Empire maintaining its territorial holdings and tributary networks. Across Eurasia, iron-working technology was becoming dominant, and the foundations of classical civilizations were being laid.

events.title: Shalmaneser IV's reign: Administrative consolidation in Assyria
events.description: Shalmaneser IV continued his reign with a focus on administrative consolidation and the maintenance of Assyrian hegemony. His rule was characterized by relative military quiescence compared to his predecessors, with emphasis on collecting tribute from vassal states and maintaining the imperial bureaucracy. This period represents the mature phase of Neo-Assyrian imperial organization, with well-established systems of provincial administration and tributary relationships. The empire's territorial extent remained stable, with no major military campaigns recorded for this year.
events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser IV's reign is well-documented; specific events in 782 BCE are not individually attested in surviving sources.

events.title: Eastern Zhou Dynasty: Feudal state competition and iron-working
events.description: The Eastern Zhou Dynasty continued its period of feudal fragmentation, with major states competing for regional dominance. Iron-working technology was becoming increasingly widespread during this period, gradually replacing bronze for tools and weapons. This technological transition would have significant implications for military capabilities and agricultural productivity. The period saw the development of more sophisticated irrigation systems and the expansion of agricultural land, supporting population growth and the emergence of larger, more complex political entities.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Eastern Zhou sites
Bronze inscriptions from Eastern Zhou states

events.certainty_note: The broad technological and political context is well-established; specific events in 782 BCE are not individually documented.

events.title: Greek Archaic period: Alphabet adoption and early colonization
events.description: The Greek Archaic period continued with the ongoing adoption of the Phoenician alphabet into Greek script. By this time, the alphabet was becoming increasingly standardized across Greek city-states, though regional variations persisted. Early Greek colonization was expanding, with settlements being established in southern Italy, Sicily, and the Black Sea region. The composition of early Greek epic poetry was likely ongoing, with the Homeric epics being compiled or composed during this era. The polis system was becoming the dominant political organization, with the development of citizen assemblies and the gradual emergence of hoplite warfare.

sources.name: Later Greek historians on the Archaic period

events.certainty_note: The broad cultural context is well-established through archaeology; specific events in 782 BCE are not individually documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The dating of Shalmaneser IV's reign has minor variations across scholarly sources, but these are within ±1 year. The precise chronology of the Greek alphabet adoption remains debated, with some scholars arguing for earlier or later dates.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 782 BCE remains heavily skewed toward the Near East and China. The absence of documentation for most of the world reflects both the limited extent of literate civilizations and the biases of modern scholarship. Assyrian records are official court documents and may not reflect the full complexity of political and social life. Chinese sources are primarily later compilations, though archaeological evidence provides some contemporary verification. Greek history relies primarily on later literary sources and archaeology.

graph_edges.note: Shalmaneser IV's reign ended in 773 BCE
